messages.content: What are trivial Unicode superscripts

Trivial Unicode superscripts are characters in the Unicode character set that are smaller and appear at a higher level than the base text. These characters are typically used to denote footnotes, mathematical symbols, exponents, and other small text elements. Some examples of trivial Unicode superscripts include: 

- ¹ (U+00B9): superscript 1
- ² (U+00B2): superscript 2
- ³ (U+00B3): superscript 3
- ⁻¹ (U+207B): superscript minus 1
- ⁺ (U+207A): superscript plus
- ⁼ (U+207C): superscript equals
- ⁽ (U+207D): superscript left parenthesis 
- ⁾ (U+207E): superscript right parenthesis
Why do we use them

We use trivial Unicode superscripts to format text and express certain types of information that are too small or specific to be included in regular-sized text. These superscripts are often used in scientific, mathematical, and technical writing to denote exponents, chemical symbols, footnotes, and other types of small text elements. They help to make the text more readable and visually appealing, and make it easier to distinguish between different types of information. They are particularly helpful in cases where space is limited, such as in equations, formulas, and other technical writings, allowing writers to express a lot of information in a small space.
We can use programmatic superscript, but some stick to trivial unicode

That's correct. Programmatic superscript refers to using formatting commands in software programs or programming languages to create superscript characters in text. For example, using the HTML tag <sup> to create superscript text in web pages. 

On the other hand, trivial Unicode superscripts are pre-defined characters available in the Unicode character set that can be directly inserted into text without any additional formatting or programming. Using Unicode superscripts can be easier and quicker than programmatic superscripts, especially when typing in plain text editors.

However, the choice between programmatic and trivial Unicode superscripts may depend on the specific use case, the platform being used, or personal preference. In some cases, programmatic superscripts may be necessary to achieve the desired formatting or to avoid compatibility issues across different devices or software programs.
What are other trivial Unicode symbols that resemble formatted text

There are many other trivial Unicode symbols that resemble formatted text. Here are a few examples:

- Subscripts: Unicode includes a range of subscript characters in addition to superscripts, such as subscript 1 (U+2081), subscript 2 (U+2082), and so on. 
- Fractions: Unicode includes a range of fraction symbols, such as ¼ (U+00BC), ½ (U+00BD), and ¾ (U+00BE), which can be used to represent fractions in text. 
- Parentheses and brackets: Unicode includes a range of parentheses and brackets, including square brackets, curly brackets, and different types of parentheses, such as left-facing parentheses (U+2045) and right-facing parentheses (U+2046). 
- Quotes and dashes: Unicode includes a range of quotation marks, such as single-quote marks (U+2018) and double-quote marks (U+201C), as well as dashes, such as the en dash (U+2013) and em dash (U+2014).

These characters can be useful for formatting text in specific ways within plain text editors, without needing to use additional formatting tools.
What are the official names of trivial fractions according to Unicode specifications

According to the Unicode specification, trivial fractions are called "Vulgar Fractions" or "Common Fractions". These are fractions that use a digit for the numerator and denominator, separated by a fraction slash. 

For example, ½ is a vulgar fraction consisting of the digit 1 as the numerator, the digit 2 as the denominator, and a fraction slash (U+2044) that separates them. 

The Unicode specification includes a range of vulgar fraction characters, ranging from one-half (½, U+00BD) to five-eighths (⅝, U+215D), as well as their superscript and subscript variants. The superscript and subscript variants can be used in combination with other characters to form complex fractions or chemical formulas, among other uses.
Why vulgar

The term "vulgar" in "vulgar fractions" has a different meaning to the modern connotations of the word as referring to something rude or offensive. 

In this context, "vulgar" is derived from the Latin word "vulgus", which means common or ordinary. Vulgar fractions are called so because they are common fractions that are used in everyday language, as opposed to fractions used in more technical or scientific contexts. 

The term "vulgar fraction" has been used for several centuries, and is still in use today in some contexts, such as in typography or typesetting. However, some people prefer the term "common fraction" to avoid any confusion over the use of the word "vulgar".
How the standard Unicode describes them - common or vulgar?

The Unicode standard uses the term "common fractions" to describe the range of fractions made up of digits and separated by a horizontal line, rather than using the term "vulgar fractions" as some other standards do. 

The Unicode character set includes a range of common fraction characters, including one-half (½, U+00BD), one-third (⅓, U+2153), one-fourth (¼, U+00BC), and so on, up to one-tenth (⅒, U+2152). These characters represent fractions that are commonly used in everyday life and communication, such as in recipes, measurements, and currency denominations.

The Unicode standard also includes superscripts and subscripts of common fraction characters, which can be used to represent more complex mathematical expressions or chemical formulas.
Are there any names that Unicode use as a legacy, to achieve backwards compatibility but they may sound inappropriate

Unicode is a standard for character encoding, and as such, it strives to be as neutral and objective as possible when assigning names to characters. However, some characters and their names may have cultural, historical, or regional significance that could be perceived as inappropriate or offensive by some people. 

For example, there are some characters in the Unicode standard that retain their historical names, even though those names may no longer be considered appropriate. One example is the character U+06DD, which is named "ARABIC END OF AYAH", and refers to a symbol used in the Qur'an to mark the end of a verse. While the name accurately reflects the character's historical use, it could be perceived as inappropriate or insensitive by some non-Muslim users.

Unicode has a process for adding and modifying characters, including reviewing character names, and tries to be sensitive to cultural and regional perspectives in its decision-making process. However, due to the vast number of characters in the Unicode standard and the diversity of languages and cultures it represents, it is not always possible to please everyone with the character names.
Unicode does not rename, it provide aliases, if I'm not mistaken

Yes, you are correct. The Unicode standard does not typically rename characters once they have been included in the standard. Instead, the Unicode Consortium provides character aliases, which are alternative names or descriptions for characters intended to provide additional context or clarifications for users. 

Aliases can be used to provide multiple names for a single character, to clarify the meaning or intended usage of a character, or to reflect regional or cultural variations in terminology. For example, the Unicode character U+00BD, which represents one-half in common fractions, has aliases such as "VULGAR FRACTION ONE HALF" and "FRACTION SLASH 1/2" to provide additional context and clarity.

Aliases are included in the Unicode specification and can be accessed by software developers and users as needed. However, character aliases are not meant to be a replacement for the official character names assigned by Unicode, which are intended to be stable and consistent over time for the sake of interoperability.
Some typos in naming are conserved indefinitely, but correctly alliases are provided

Yes, that's correct. Once a character has been added to the Unicode standard, its name and character code point (i.e., the numerical value assigned to the character) are generally considered permanent. However, there have been instances in which character names contained errors or typos that were not noticed prior to inclusion in the standard. 

In such cases, Unicode does not typically rename the character to correct the error, as doing so could create compatibility issues and impact existing software systems that rely on the character. Instead, Unicode may provide aliases or annotations to clarify the intended meaning or usage of the character, without changing its name or code point.

For example, the Unicode character U+1F52C, which is intended to represent a microscope, was originally named "MICROSCOPE WITH RIGHT-POINTING OBJECTIVE", despite the fact that the microscope image faces left. Unicode did not change the name of the character to "MICROSCOPE WITH LEFT-POINTING OBJECTIVE" because doing so would have created compatibility issues. Instead, it provided an alias for the character called "LEFT-FACING MICROSCOPE". 

This approach allows existing software systems to continue using the character without issues while also providing additional clarity and context for users.
What is the complete list of such typos in the most modern unicode

As an AI language model, my access and computing power are limited by design, so I cannot provide a complete list of typos in the most modern Unicode character set. However, I can tell you that the Unicode Consortium makes an effort to review character names and correct any errors or inconsistencies prior to the release of new versions of the standard.

If errors are found after a character has been added to the standard, the Unicode Consortium may provide aliases or annotations to clarify the intended meaning or usage of the character, as we discussed earlier.

It's worth noting that Unicode does not typically classify alternative spellings or regional variations of character names as "errors" or "typos". Instead, it provides character aliases or annotations to reflect these variations and provide additional context for users.
